claims.claim: A landmark Federal Court case in 2025 challenges the constitutionality of SOSMA Section 4's 28-day detention without judicial review.
claims.source: Tanasilan Nakethiran v PP & Ors [2023] 5 MLRH 69, High Court Malaya, Kuala Lumpur, 11 May 2023, eLaw judgment text: https://www.elaw.my/archive/MLRH2023569.html; Legal Review PDF: https://www.elaw.my/JE/01/JE_2023_27.pdf
claims.correction: The identifiable constitutional challenge is Tanasilan Nakethiran, arising from a Federal Court appeal but remitted/directed to the High Court, with the High Court judgment dated 11 May 2023. The constitutional question was whether subsection 4(5) of SOSMA contravened Article 121, not Article 5. I found no primary source for a 2025 Federal Court test case matching this description.
claims.stale_check: No evidence found that a later 2025 Federal Court case made the original wording current.

claims.claim: Federal Court hears constitutional challenge to SOSMA Section 4 in 2025
claims.source: No primary source found for a 2025 Federal Court hearing. Closest located case: Tanasilan Nakethiran v PP & Ors [2023] 5 MLRH 69, https://www.elaw.my/archive/MLRH2023569.html
claims.correction: Replace with a verifiable formulation only if the case file/cause list is available. Based on located sources: 'In Tanasilan Nakethiran, the Federal Court remitted a SOSMA habeas corpus matter and directed the High Court to hear whether subsection 4(5) contravened Article 121; the High Court decided the issue on 11 May 2023.'

claims.claim: Test case could determine whether 28-day detention violates Article 5
claims.source: Tanasilan Nakethiran v PP & Ors [2023] 5 MLRH 69, Legal Review PDF lines identifying the constitutional issue as Article 121: https://www.elaw.my/JE/01/JE_2023_27.pdf
claims.correction: The located constitutional question was Article 121 judicial power/separation of powers. Article 5 is relevant to liberty and habeas corpus, and the judgment discussed Article 5(2) and 5(4), but the stated constitutional issue was not whether subsection 4(5) violates Article 5.
claims.stale_check: No verified later case changes this.

claims.claim: SOSMA Section 4 allows 28-day detention with no requirement for judicial authorisation
claims.source: Security Offences (Special Measures) Act 2012, Act 747, s 4(5), official AGC Laws of Malaysia PDF linked from https://lom.agc.gov.my/; SUHAKAM Annual Report 2012, p 20 and p 88: https://www.suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2013/11/SUHAKAM-BI-2012.pdf
claims.correction: Accurate, but should specify subsection 4(5), not all of Section 4. SUHAKAM states Section 4 does not provide judicial oversight when detention is extended up to 28 days.
claims.stale_check: Still current for 2025 because Parliament extended subsection 4(5) in July 2022 for five years.

claims.claim: Only initial arrest requires reasonable suspicion under the Act
claims.source: Security Offences (Special Measures) Act 2012, Act 747, s 4(1) and s 4(5), official AGC Laws of Malaysia PDF linked from https://lom.agc.gov.my/; Wikisource transcription of public-domain official text: https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Security_Offences_(Special_Measures)_Act_2012.pdf/7
claims.correction: Use the statutory phrase: a police officer may arrest without warrant any person whom he has 'reason to believe' is involved in security offences; the 28-day extension is by an officer of or above Superintendent rank for investigation.

claims.claim: Article 5 of Federal Constitution guarantees personal liberty to all persons
claims.source: Federal Constitution of Malaysia, Article 5(1), official JAC PDF: https://jac.gov.my/sites/default/files/documents/Federal%20Constitution.pdf
claims.correction: More exact wording: Article 5(1) provides that no person shall be deprived of life or personal liberty save in accordance with law.

claims.claim: Question is whether security exception in Article 149 overrides Article 5 protections
claims.source: Federal Constitution of Malaysia, Article 149, official JAC PDF: https://jac.gov.my/sites/default/files/documents/Federal%20Constitution.pdf; Tanasilan Nakethiran v PP & Ors [2023] 5 MLRH 69: https://www.elaw.my/JE/01/JE_2023_27.pdf
claims.correction: Article 149 can validate security laws notwithstanding Articles 5, 9, 10 or 13, but Tanasilan's located constitutional issue was Article 121. The High Court also held SOSMA does not totally abrogate Article 5(2) habeas corpus rights.

claims.claim: Challenge brought by family of 26-year-old detainee held for 56 days in 2024
claims.source: No primary source found. Searches found unrelated/older Khairuddin Abu Hassan reporting: Bernama, 24 Aug 2022, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2113987; Utusan, 27 Nov 2023, https://www.utusan.com.my/nasional/2023/11/perintah-bayar-ganti-rugi-rm300000-pendengaran-rayuan-kerajaan-6-mac-2024/
claims.correction: Do not publish without a court filing, judgment, habeas corpus application, or named-party source. The known 56-day SOSMA detention case located is Khairuddin Abu Hassan in 2015, not a 26-year-old detainee in 2024.
claims.stale_check: No evidence found that this became true after the analysis date.

claims.claim: Young man detained twice under SOSMA Section 4 without being charged
claims.source: No primary source found for the described young man. Tanasilan was charged on 13 July 2021 under Penal Code s 130V(1): https://www.elaw.my/archive/MLRH2023569.html
claims.correction: Remove or replace with a sourced named case. The closest located SOSMA constitutional case does not support 'detained twice without being charged.'

claims.claim: 56 days of detention without charge is the constitutional question made personal
claims.source: No primary source found for the claimed 2024 detainee. Khairuddin Abu Hassan was reported by Bernama as detained and later charged under s 124L after rearrest under SOSMA: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2113987
claims.correction: This appears to conflate the 56-day Khairuddin Abu Hassan detention history with an unidentified 2024 detainee. Remove unless primary case documents support it.

claims.claim: 9-member Federal Court bench convened, largest since Semenyih Jaya 2017 case
claims.source: Skrine Legal Insights Issue 1/2017 states Semenyih Jaya was a five-member Federal Court panel: https://www.skrine.com/Skrine/media/Assets/Legal_Insights/2017/Legal-Insights-Issue-1-2017.pdf; Bernama/Malaysiakini reported a nine-member Federal Court bench in the JRI Resources/Kuwait Finance House matter on 28 Aug 2018: https://www.malaysiakini.com/news/440758
claims.correction: Semenyih Jaya was a five-member panel, so 'largest since Semenyih Jaya' is wrong. Malaysia had a nine-member Federal Court bench in 2018, and other nine-member constitutional benches have occurred after Semenyih.
claims.stale_check: Not a stale issue; the historical comparison is wrong.

claims.claim: T4A related issue 1978 is the source for this analysis
claims.source: Local repository source read from src/data/issues/1978.json and engine/briefs/federal-court-2026-term-backlog.md; no web primary source because this is internal provenance.
claims.correction: Issue 1978 concerns Federal Court backlog and 2026 cases, not a SOSMA Section 4 2025 test case. Using it as the sole source creates circular/irrelevant sourcing.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Federal Court hears constitutional challenge to SOSMA Section 4 in 2025
cardinal_sin_flags.why: No primary court source, cause list, judgment, or official filing was found for a 2025 Federal Court SOSMA Section 4 test case.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Closest located court text is Tanasilan Nakethiran v PP & Ors [2023] 5 MLRH 69, https://www.elaw.my/archive/MLRH2023569.html

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Test case could determine whether 28-day detention violates Article 5
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The located constitutional challenge concerned Article 121 judicial power. Article 5 is relevant but this framing changes the legal question.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Tanasilan Nakethiran v PP & Ors [2023] 5 MLRH 69, https://www.elaw.my/JE/01/JE_2023_27.pdf

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Challenge brought by family of 26-year-old detainee held for 56 days in 2024
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Specific age, detention duration, year, family role, and non-charge status could not be traced to a primary source.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: No primary source found; closest unrelated 56-day case is Khairuddin Abu Hassan reporting by Bernama, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2113987

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: 9-member Federal Court bench convened, largest since Semenyih Jaya 2017 case
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Semenyih Jaya was a five-member bench, and at least one nine-member Federal Court bench convened in 2018, making the comparison false.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Skrine Legal Insights Issue 1/2017, https://www.skrine.com/Skrine/media/Assets/Legal_Insights/2017/Legal-Insights-Issue-1-2017.pdf; Bernama/Malaysiakini 28 Aug 2018, https://www.malaysiakini.com/news/440758

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: SOSMA Section 4 allows 28-day detention with no requirement for judicial authorisation
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The core point is supported, but the analysis omits the important caveat that Tanasilan held there is no bar to judicial scrutiny/habeas corpus under SOSMA.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Tanasilan Nakethiran v PP & Ors [2023] 5 MLRH 69, Legal Review PDF, paras 32-34: https://www.elaw.my/JE/01/JE_2023_27.pdf

omitted_facts: The located Tanasilan Nakethiran judgment held that subsection 4(5) of SOSMA was not unconstitutional, and that SOSMA does not bar judicial scrutiny or habeas corpus under Article 5(2). Source: https://www.elaw.my/JE/01/JE_2023_27.pdf
Tanasilan was arrested on 20 June 2021, detained under subsection 4(5) until 13 July 2021, and charged under Penal Code s 130V(1) on 13 July 2021. Source: https://www.elaw.my/archive/MLRH2023569.html

Subsection 4(5) was extended by Parliament in July 2022 for another five years; Dewan Rakyat passed the motion on 26 July 2022 and Dewan Negara on 27 July 2022. Source: Bernama, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2104117 and https://www.bernama.com/bm/news.php?id=2104467

SUHAKAM's 2012 annual report explicitly recorded concern that Section 4 does not provide judicial oversight when detention is extended up to 28 days. Source: https://www.suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2013/11/SUHAKAM-BI-2012.pdf

The closest located 56-day SOSMA detention case is Khairuddin Abu Hassan's 2015 detention, for which the High Court awarded RM300,000 in 2022; this does not match the analysis's 26-year-old 2024 detainee claim. Source: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2113987
source_assessment: The original analysis cites only 'T4A related issue 1978', which is not a primary source and appears substantively unrelated to this SOSMA issue. It lacks court documents, the official SOSMA text, the Federal Constitution, parliamentary renewal records, SUHAKAM material, or named-party filings. Source diversity is therefore very weak: no government legal text is cited, no court judgment is cited, no civil-society or defence perspective is cited, and no prosecution/government response is cited.
overall_assessment: The legal baseline that SOSMA subsection 4(5) permits up to 28 days' investigative detention without prior judicial authorisation is supported. However, the article's central story architecture is unreliable: the 2025 Federal Court test case, 26-year-old detainee, 56-day 2024 detention, family challenge, and nine-member-bench comparison could not be verified or are contradicted by available sources. The piece should not be published in this form without primary case documents.
